# Development Guide / Development Guide

This document is for maintainers, contributors, and AI Agents that will collaborate on this project later. The README is only the public entry point; modules, skills, local overlays, release validation, and AI collaboration rules are kept here.

## Product surface rule / Product surface rule

The README is the product entry point, not a maintenance log, personal explanation, or decision draft. It should only include: project positioning, installation, common commands, core directories, boundaries, and documentation entry points.

Put complex background, design details, private/local overlays, and AI collaboration prompts in `docs/` or LLL workspace reports.

## CLI design / CLI design

The CLI has two layers:

| Layer | User-facing examples | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Product commands | `aios status`, `aios doctor`, `aios update`, `aios update skills`, `aios update modules lins-living-loop` | Stable commands for normal users |
| Expert subdomains | `aios skillpack sync --apply`, `aios skillpack dev-link --apply`, `aios assets doctor` | Manifest reconciliation, development, debugging |

`skillpack sync` means “reconcile/converge runtime skills according to `skillpack.yaml`”; it is not a normal update. Normal users should prefer:

```bash
aios update skills
```

`aios update` is equivalent to `aios update all` by default. Use these commands when you need more granularity:

```bash
aios update modules
aios update modules lins-living-loop
aios update skills
aios update ops
```

An update target only deserves to become an independent subject when it has its own lifecycle, takes significant time, has failure risk, or represents a clear user intent. For now, `modules`, `skills`, and `ops` are enough.

## Global command installation / Global command installation

The installer always creates:

```text
~/aios/bin/aios -> ~/aios/modules/aios-kit/aios
```

Users are recommended to add `~/aios/bin` to PATH:

```bash
export PATH="$HOME/aios/bin:$PATH"
```

If a user wants to write directly into an existing PATH directory, they can use:

```bash
bash install.sh --global-bin ~/.local/bin
```

The installer will not overwrite an existing conflicting `~/.local/bin/aios`.

## Adding a portable skill / Adding a portable skill

A skill suitable for the portable base pack should meet at least one of these conditions:

- Most AIOS users will need it frequently;
- It significantly improves the default post-install capabilities while having few side effects;
- It is strongly related to core AIOS workflows, such as skill discovery, document workflows, LLL, and resource/project resolution.

Steps:

1. Add the entry to `skillpack.yaml`, clearly specifying `source`, `skill`, and `reason`.
2. Run a dry run:

   ```bash
   aios skillpack sync --dry-run
   aios update skills --dry-run
   ```

3. Install and validate:

   ```bash
   aios update skills
   aios doctor
   ```

4. Perform a fresh HOME smoke install to avoid results being polluted by existing local files.
5. Commit/push after passing the public audit.

## Adding a first-party skill / Adding a first-party skill

If the skill is maintained by AIOS itself, prefer one of the following truth-source locations:

| Case | Truth source | Runtime install |
|---|---|---|
| Small AIOS-specific skill | `aios-kit/skills/<skill>` | copy for users, symlink for dev |
| Independent product-like skill | separate repo under `~/aios/modules/<repo>` | copy for users, symlink for dev |
| Subskill inside a template repo | `<repo>/skills/<skill>` | copy for users, symlink for dev |

Do not treat the runtime skills directory as the truth source. On development machines, you may symlink individual runtime skills to Git worktrees, but public installs should copy by default.

## Adding a module / Adding a module

A module is an updatable source-code or template checkout under `~/aios/modules/<name>`. Suitable module targets usually have independent lifecycles, such as LLL, an OPS vault template, or future multi-device interconnection modules.

When adding a module, consider:

- Is it part of the public portable base, or a local overlay for this machine?
- Does the installer need to clone it?
- Is `aios update modules <name>` enough, or does it need additional refresh steps?
- Does it provide runtime skills? If so, where should the runtime skills be copied/symlinked?

If it is only a single skill, do not turn it into a module too early. Put it in `skillpack.yaml` or `aios-kit/skills/<skill>` first.

## Local overlay policy / Local overlay policy

Local overlays are for a maintainer’s own machine, private infrastructure, central control plane, or experimental modules. They may belong to “Lin’s AIOS”, but they are not part of the public portable base pack.

Current local overlay examples:

| Skill | Location | Why local-only now |
|---|---|---|
| `cloud-server-ssh-assets` | `skillpack.local.yaml` | Bound to Lin's cloud server inventory and SSH/resource conventions |
| `central-agent-control-plane` | `skillpack.local.yaml` / Hermes profile | Bound to Lin's central Hermes/control-plane operations |

If multi-device interconnection, central Agents, or remote execution become public core AIOS capabilities in the future, they should be abstracted into portable modules/skills that only expose generic workflows, schemas, and templates, not private resource facts.

## AIOps skills distinction / AIOps skills distinction

`aiops-vault` is the entry/companion skill for the OPS vault. It defines how to read the vault, respect secret boundaries, maintain `resources.md`, `maintenance-log.jsonl`, `secrets-location.md`, and so on. Its `SKILL.md` is located at the root of the `aiops-vault-template` repository, so on development machines the runtime symlink points to the repository root:

```text
~/.agents/skills/aiops-vault -> ~/projects/aiops-vault-template
```

`aiops-service-operations` is a narrower service operations workflow skill, located in a subdirectory of the template repository:

```text
~/.agents/skills/aiops-service-operations -> ~/projects/aiops-vault-template/skills/aiops-service-operations
```

## How to ask an AI to add a module / How to ask an AI to add a module

```text
I made a new module: <module name>.
Local path: <path>.
Goal: include it in AIOS's portable install/update flow.
Please determine whether it should be a portable base, first-party skill, independent module, or local overlay.
Requirements: do not copy my private data or secrets; do not take over a friend's entire existing skills directory; the README should only contain information needed by general users; development rules should go in docs/development.md; run dry-run, doctor, public audit, and a fresh HOME smoke install; after passing, commit and push.
```

## Skillpack update semantics / Skillpack update semantics

`aios-kit` is more conservative than simply “adding everything again”:

- Each managed skill records its installation path and local content hash;
- Before updating, if a runtime skill is found to have been modified locally by the user, overwriting is refused by default;
- Overwrite only with explicit `--force`;
- Stale skills are removed only with `--prune --apply`;
- Prune only deletes paths recorded in install-state as managed by `aios-kit`.

Development machines can use symlink mode so that edits the agent makes to runtime skills land in a Git-visible worktree:

```bash
cd ~/projects/aios-kit
./aios skillpack dev-link --apply
./aios skillpack doctor
```

Normal user/friend installs use copy mode by default:

```bash
./aios skillpack sync --apply
```

`--apply` and `--dry-run` are mutually exclusive; default low-level skillpack/assets commands are no-op unless `--apply` is passed.

## Release checklist / Release checklist

Before release, run at least:

```bash
bash -n install.sh
python3 -m py_compile scripts/aios.py scripts/audit_public.py
python3 scripts/audit_public.py
aios update --dry-run
aios update skills --dry-run
aios update modules --dry-run
aios doctor

tmp_home="$(mktemp -d)"
./aios --home "$tmp_home" init --dry-run
./aios --home "$tmp_home" status

git status --short
```

When changes involve the installer, skillpack, module cloning, or runtime skill paths, you must perform a fresh HOME smoke install.
